calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR COURT
Off Calendar Sep-25-2020 Vacated

Plaintiff Ghilotti Bros. Inc.'S Motion To Consolidate With Cgc-17-560034 (Stipulated Continuance)
Off Calendar Aug-12-2020 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: Plaintiff Ghilotti Bros. Inc.'s Motion To Consolidate With CGC-17-560034.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling: Plaintiff and Cross-Defendant Ghilotti Bros., Inc.'s motion to consolidate for all purposes is granted. The two cases involve the same parties, arise out of a common set of facts relating to the parties' performance under a subcontract for a construction project, and raise overlapping claims and issues. Consolidation would further efficiency, prevent duplicative litigation, and avoid inconsistent rulings. That Ghilotti Bros. has not filed its motion in the lowest numbered case makes no practical difference whatever. The parties can address the trial date in Dept. 206.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/EPS)

MASTER CALENDAR COURT
Off Calendar Jun-22-2020 Continued To Feb-01-21 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

MOTION TO CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS OF CASE(S) CGC-17-560034 WITH CASE CGC-19-576488
Off Calendar Apr-02-2020 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-05-2019 Set For Trial On Jun-22-20 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Oct-22-2019 Continued To Dec-18-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-05-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SYNERGY PROJECT MANAGEMENT, INC.'S DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFF'S COMPLAINT.

Defendant Synergy Project Management, Inc.'s demurrer is overruled in its entirety.

The demurrer to the breach of contract cause of action is overruled. Plaintiff is not required to attach a copy of the contract or plead its terms verbatim to state a claim. (Construction Protective Services, Inc. v. TIG Specialty Ins. Co. (2002) 29 Cal.4th 189, 199 ["a plaintiff may plead the legal effect of the contract rather than its precise language."]; Scolinos v. Kolts (1995) 37 Cal.App.4th 635, 640 ["A contract should be pleaded either in haec verba or according to its legal intendment and effect."].) To plead a contract according to its legal effect, the complaining party should allege the making and then proceed to allege the substance of its relevant terms. (See Pneucrete Corp. v. United States Fidelity & Guaranty Co., (1935) 7 Cal.App.2d 733, 741-742; McKell v. Washington Mut. Inc. (2006) 142 Cal.App.4th 1457, 1489.) Here, plaintiff alleges the substance of the relevant terms and states a claim. (Complaint, 9-13.) Plaintiff's failure to attach the contract to the complaint even though it is referenced as an exhibit is a minor error that does not render the complaint uncertain.

The demurrer to the breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing claim is overruled. While there appears to be some overlap between this claim and the breach of contract claim, the allegations do not show that the implied covenant claim merely restates the breach of contract claim. Because the claim is not clearly superfluous, the demurrer is overruled.

The demurrer to the breach of the contractual indemnity provision is overruled. "Although indemnity generally relates to third party claims, 'this general rule does not apply if the parties to a contract use the term 'indemnity' to include direct liability as well as third party liability.'" (Zalkind v. Ceradyne, Inc. (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th1010, 1024.) Here, the indemnity clause provides that "[t]o the greatest extent permitted by law, [Defendant] shall indemnify and hold harmless [Plaintiff] from and against all damages, costs, losses, and expenses." (Complaint, 37.) This language is broad enough to include a direct indemnity claim. (See Hot Rods, LLC v. Northrop Grumman Sys.Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 1166, 1181-1182 ["if the language was meant only to cover third party claims, there seems little point in including the phrase 'damages, costs, losses' when those would all have been covered already?The extremely broad language here, such as 'any claims, ... damages, costs, losses' does not suggest the parties intended to limit the provision to third party claims."] The contract is reasonably susceptible to Plaintiff's interpretation and the court accepts that interpretation on demurrer. (See Aragon-Haas v. Family Security Ins. Services, Inc. (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 232, 239 ["While plaintiffs' interpretation of the contract may prove invalid, it [is] improper to resolve the issue against her solely on her own pleading. 'In ruling on a demurrer, the likelihood that the pleading will be able to prove his allegations is not the question.'"].)

A Proposed Order is submitted by Email.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)
